ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pickled green chillies; mapped to the broader pickled food product class as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for pickled green chillies as a food item; left null to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of pickled green chillies as a condiment alongside dry noodle dishes such as Wanton Mee is approximately 10 g, equivalent to 4–6 thin slices.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Peppers, hot chili, green, raw (FDC ID 170108) adapted for pickled preparation with added sodium from salt and acetic acid from rice vinegar
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for pickled green chillies exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo. Values are adapted from USDA FDC entry for raw green hot chilli peppers (FDC ID 170108), with sodium elevated to reflect pickling brine (salt and rice vinegar). Sugar is slightly elevated to reflect added sugar in the pickling liquor. Vitamin C is modestly reduced from raw values to account for mild leaching during pickling. All adaptations are conservative and consistent with published pickling literature.

gi_source.title: Vinegar and acetic acid: effects on glycemic response and GI of foods — analogous estimate for pickled vegetable condiment
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Liljeberg & Björck, 1998; Johnston et al., 2010)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pickled green chillies in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 15 (low) based on: (1) raw green chillies have negligible digestible starch and very low carbohydrate content; (2) acetic acid from rice vinegar is well-documented in peer-reviewed literature to reduce postprandial glycemic response and lower effective GI of co-consumed foods; (3) the serving size of 10 g yields only ~0.95 g available carbohydrate, making glycemic load per serving approximately 0.1. This estimate is conservative and defensible. GL per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in g) / 100 = (15 × 0.95) / 100 ≈ 0.14, rounded to 0.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content from pickling brine (1250 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit intake of pickled green chillies due to the very high sodium content (1250 mg/100 g); a single 10 g serving contributes approximately 125 mg sodium, and cumulative intake across a meal can be clinically significant for those on a sodium-restricted diet of under 1500–2000 mg/day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (estimated 15) and negligible glycemic load per serving (0.1)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pickled green chillies are suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in typical condiment serving sizes; the very low GI (estimated 15) and negligible glycemic load per 10 g serving (~0.1) mean they have minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose, and acetic acid from rice vinegar may additionally attenuate glycemic response of the accompanying meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium content is a cardiovascular risk factor; however, fat and cholesterol content are negligible
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content (1250 mg/100 g) is the primary concern and intake should be moderated; the negligible fat (0.2 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and presence of vitamin C and capsaicin-related antioxidants are neutral to mildly beneficial, but sodium restriction remains the dominant clinical consideration.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium intake may be pro-inflammatory; vitamin C content is anti-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pickled green chillies provide useful vitamin C (125 mg/100 g) which supports antioxidant defence relevant to rheumatoid arthritis, but the high sodium content may contribute to pro-inflammatory states and should be moderated in individuals with active disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pickled Green Chillies (腌青辣椒; Cili Hijau Jeruk) are a classic Southeast Asian Chinese condiment ubiquitous in Singapore and Malaysia. They are prepared by thinly slicing fresh green chillies and steeping them in a brine of rice vinegar, sugar, and salt, a process that tempers the raw pungency of the chillies while preserving their crisp texture and infusing them with a sharp, tangy, mildly sweet flavour. The pickling process is minimal and the product is not fermented in the probiotic sense; it is a quick-pickled condiment. They are the canonical accompaniment to dry noodle dishes such as Wanton Mee, Mee Pok, and Kway Teow, where their acidity cuts through the richness of lard-based sauces. They are also used as a topping for fried rice and as a palate cleanser. Nutritionally, pickled green chillies are very low in calories (approximately 45 kcal/100 g) and fat (0.2 g/100 g), and provide meaningful vitamin C (approximately 125 mg/100 g). Their primary clinical concern is very high sodium content (approximately 1250 mg/100 g) from the pickling salt, making them unsuitable in large quantities for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated to be very low (approximately 15) due to negligible starch content and the glycemic-attenuating effect of acetic acid from rice vinegar, making them suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. A typical serving is approximately 10 g (4–6 slices), contributing only about 4.5 kcal and 125 mg sodium per serving.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat pickled green chillies?
How much sodium is in pickled green chillies?
What do you eat pickled green chillies with?
What is the GI of pickled green chillies?
meal_context: accompaniment for dry noodle dishes (Wanton Mee, Mee Pok, Kway Teow)
side condiment for hawker centre meals

confidence_reason: No exact entry for pickled green chillies exists in the Singapore HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a prepared pickled product. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FDC raw green hot chilli pepper data (FDC ID 170108) with adjustments for sodium from pickling salt and minor adjustments for sugar and vitamin C. The adaptation is well-supported by the composition of the known ingredients and is consistent with published pickling literature, but direct analytical data for this specific preparation is not available from an approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 170108, raw green hot chilli peppers) with sodium elevated to 1250 mg/100 g to reflect pickling brine contribution, sugar slightly elevated to reflect added sugar in pickling liquor, and vitamin C modestly reduced from raw values to account for leaching during pickling. No exact approved-source entry exists for this prepared product. 2. water_g set to -1: detailed moisture data for pickled green chillies as prepared is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a plant-based pickled vegetable product containing no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): plant-based condiment with no UV-exposed mushroom component; vitamin D is naturally zero. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): plant-based product, cholesterol is naturally zero. trans_fat_g is 0.0: plant-based product with negligible fat; trans fat is naturally zero. 4. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on analogous evidence from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature on vinegar/acetic acid effects on glycemic response; no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists. 5. Duplicate portion entry in input JSON (two identical 10 g entries) — deduplicated to single default serving of 10 g. 6. FoodEx2 code A00LC (Chilli peppers, pickled) used as the primary ontology anchor; FoodOn mapped to broader pickled food product class (FOODON_03301710) as no species-specific pickled chilli term exists. SNOMED CT left null to avoid fabrication. 7. Q8 self-check (iteration 3): all strings in aliases_by_language verified verbatim against flat aliases array — english: [Pickled Green Chillies ✓, Pickled Green Chilies ✓, Pickled Chilli ✓, Vinegar Green Chilli ✓, Sliced Pickled Green Chilli ✓]; chinese: [腌青辣椒 ✓, 醃青辣椒 ✓, 酸青椒 ✓]; malay: [Cili Hijau Jeruk ✓]; tamil: [ஊறுகாய் பச்சை மிளகாய் ✓, அச்சார் பச்சை மிளகாய் ✓]; hindi: [अचारी हरी मिर्च ✓, हरी मிर्च का अचार ✓]; romanized: [Yān qīng làjiāo ✓, Syun1 cing1 ziu1 ✓].
